request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"38-studios::gaming","name":"38 Studios","one_liner":"Celebrity-founded US game studio that built an AAA MMO and single-player RPG on a $75 M state loan, collapsed in 2012 when Kingdoms of Amalur: Reckoning sold 1.3 M copies against a 2 M break-even, leaving Project Copernicus unfinished.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"38 Studios monetized via one-time retail purchase (boxed/digital sale of Kingdoms of Amalur: Reckoning at a standard AAA price point). The new pitch is free-to-play with cosmetics and a battle pass — a fundamentally different revenue model that trades upfront purchase revenue for recurring, lower-friction micro-transactions. Almost no overlap."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are US-based consumer gaming products targeting the same broad gaming audience. However, 38 Studios focused on PC/console MMO and single-player RPG, while the new pitch targets mobile (iOS/Android) plus Steam with a 4X strategy sub-genre. Overlapping addressable market (gamers) but different sub-genre and platform mix."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on celebrity founder credibility, a high-profile IP co-created with R.A. Salvatore and Todd McFarlane, and a traditional publisher distribution deal (EA Partners) to reach retail audiences. The new pitch implies a direct digital distribution GTM (app stores + Steam) with no mention of a publisher intermediary — a meaningfully different go-to-market path."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios was already operating at large scale (400 employees, $118 M+ in operating expenses, state-backed financing) before shipping a single title. The new pitch appears to be an early-stage studio. Both are pre-revenue at concept stage in different ways, but the scale and capital requirements are orders of magnitude apart."}},"why_similar":"Both are video game studios building original IP for a consumer audience in the US gaming market, relying on a single flagship title to validate the studio and generate revenue. Both face the core challenge of funding long development cycles before seeing a single dollar of player revenue, and both are operating in competitive genres where differentiation is difficult (MMO/RPG then; 4X strategy now). The underlying business risk — spending heavily before launch against uncertain sales projections — is structurally identical.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization model: 38 Studios used a one-time retail purchase; the new pitch uses free-to-play with cosmetics and a battle pass, which lowers acquisition friction and creates recurring revenue streams instead of a single launch-window event. 2. Platform mix: 38 Studios targeted PC/console; the new pitch targets iOS, Android, and Steam, with mobile as the primary distribution channel. 3. Scale and capital structure: 38 Studios burned $118 M+ across 400 employees funded by a state loan; the new pitch does not imply comparable headcount or public financing. 4. Genre: 38 Studios built an MMORPG and action RPG; the new pitch is a 4X strategy title — a different design and retention challenge. 5. Publisher dependency: 38 Studios required an EA Partners publishing deal; the new pitch distributes directly via app stores and Steam.","failure_causes":["Critically undercapitalized relative to project scope — needed 2 M unit sales just to break even on Reckoning","Single-title revenue dependency with no recurring or diversified income","Over-ambitious MMO as flagship product in a declining MMORPG market","State-backed loan as primary capital source created political and legal fragility","Founder lacked business experience, leading to organizational dysfunction","Sales of launch title (1.3 M copies) fell short of break-even threshold despite critical acclaim","Inability to secure follow-on investment when launch underperformed"],"lessons_for_input":["Design your monetization model so that no single sales milestone determines studio survival — recurring battle-pass and cosmetics revenue provides more runway than a one-time launch window","Validate player engagement and willingness-to-spend with a soft launch or MVP before committing to full production scope","Keep headcount and burn rate proportional to proven revenue, not projected revenue","Avoid single points of funding failure (e.g., one government loan, one publisher deal) — diversify capital sources early","Set realistic break-even unit/player thresholds and model downside scenarios explicitly before greenlighting production"]}
